name: Sphinx of Thebes
description: The Sphinx of Thebes, a fearsome creature in Greek mythology, was a monstrous hybrid with the body of a lion, the wings of an eagle, and the face of a woman. Sent by the gods (often attributed to Hera or Ares), she terrorized the city of Thebes, preying on travelers who could not answer her riddle. The Sphinx perched atop a mountain overlooking Thebes, demanding that all who passed solve her enigmatic question: 'What walks on four legs in the morning, two in the afternoon, and three in the evening?' Those who failed to answer correctly were devoured. The Sphinx continued her reign of terror until Oedipus arrived and correctly answered the riddle: 'Man.' Infuriated and defeated, the Sphinx cast herself from her high perch, plunging to her death. Her demise freed Thebes from her tyranny, marking a significant moment in the Oedipus mythos and highlighting the power of intellect and human ingenuity against monstrous forces.
subtitle: Guardian of Thebes and Riddle Master
appearance: The Sphinx of Thebes possesses the body of a powerful lion, complete with sharp claws and a muscular build. She has large, feathered wings resembling those of an eagle, allowing her to soar through the air. Her most distinctive feature is her human head, typically depicted as that of a beautiful woman, although sometimes portrayed with more bestial features. Her gaze is piercing and intelligent, reflecting her cunning nature.
habitat: The mountains surrounding the city of Thebes, specifically a high perch overlooking the city.
behavior: The Sphinx is a cunning and cruel creature, delighting in tormenting and devouring those who cannot answer her riddle. She is fiercely intelligent and takes pride in her intellectual superiority. She is also depicted as stubborn and unwilling to yield until her riddle is solved, demonstrating a possessive nature over her position as gatekeeper of Thebes.

keyMyths.title: Oedipus and the Sphinx
keyMyths.description: Oedipus encountered the Sphinx and successfully answered her riddle. This led to the Sphinx's demise and Oedipus's subsequent rise to power in Thebes.

sources.author: Sophocles
sources.description: A tragic play that features the encounter between Oedipus and the Sphinx.
sources.text: A tragic play that features the encounter between Oedipus and the Sphinx.

sources.author: Apollodorus
